children.value: is a spatial predictive inference paradigm implemented in PsychoPy. On each trial, participants allocate up to 7 defensive nets across 32 evenly spaced angular positions on a circular perimeter, with the goal of intercepting a mosquito that will subsequently attack from a direction governed by the task’s latent structure.
The task is presented with a narrative and visual framing (described in

) in which the participant is a defender protecting a central resource from mosquito attacks. The spatial, circular structure of the defense problem directly embodies the angular inference problem that is the scientific focus of the study.
The central visual display consists of:
that changes color each trial to indicate the active cue identity. During the anticipation phase, this cue is visible; during barrier placement, it remains visible.
on which the 32 slots are positioned at equal angular intervals (11.25° per slot).
- small white circles at each slot position indicating available placement positions.
- a colored arc on an inner circle indicating the current blood point total (initially 100, updated after each outcome trial).
- image-based net stimuli that appear at slot positions when barriers are placed.
- a small red circle showing the current cursor position, accompanied by a small net image and a counter showing remaining nets.
: what is generated in the PsychoPy code vs what is pregenerated and imported as images
two latent environmental states, characterized by a 120° shift relative to each other, sequential alternating
~ 10% oddballs, also 120° shift in relation to the base direction (defined by the current latent environmental state and the color cue in the respective trial)
Each trial consists of four sequential phases:
The color cue appears at the center of the screen. No net placement is permitted during this phase. The cursor is centered at the start of each trial.
: The anticipation phase gives participants time to form their prediction about the attack direction and plan their net placement, without allowing pre-commitment.
Participants use the mouse to place nets on the perimeter slots. The mouse cursor is visible and interactive during this phase.
: Place one net at the nearest slot to the cursor position.
: Remove one net from the slot nearest the cursor (if the slot contains at least one net).
: 7 per trial. Participants may place fewer; they do not need to use all 7.
: Multiple nets may be placed in the same slot, up to 7. The defense effectiveness of stacked nets follows a cubic decline function (see
: 7.0 seconds. Visual countdown is not displayed, but audio beeps sound at 3s, 2s, and 1s remaining.
: The phase ends early if all 7 nets are placed (though participants may continue repositioning until time expires or they press no further keys).
The cursor display during placement shows: a small net image offset from the cursor, a counter displaying remaining nets (e.g.,
), and a hover highlight on the nearest slot.

uses nearest-slot logic: the clicked position maps to whichever of the 32 slots is closest. Slots are positioned at radius 8° from center; clicks anywhere on the screen are mapped to the nearest slot.
A jittered delay between net placement and outcome delivery. The color cue remains visible. No new interactions are possible.
: Drawn from a truncated exponential distribution:

children.value: : Prevents participants from anticipating outcome onset from a fixed delay; adds temporal jitter for physiological data analysis.
The outcome phase varies depending on whether the current trial is an

The mosquito approaches from the attack angle, animated over 2.3 seconds. The mosquito image is selected based on the attack direction (8 directional variants: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW). The mosquito buzz sound plays throughout the animation.
: If the mosquito encounters a net in its approach slot, it reverses and flies back. The
function displays a green sparkle animation, “+1 ♥” rising animation, and “BLOCKED!” text. Blood points increase by 1.
: The mosquito reaches the center. The
function displays a red flash overlay, screen shake effect, “-1 ♥” falling animation, and “BITE!” text. Blood points decrease by 1.
Hit probability is determined by the net count in the attack slot:
This function follows a cubic decline:

phit(7)=0p_{\text{hit}}(7) = 0phit​(7)=0

children.value: No mosquito animation is shown. A brief “...” display appears for 2.3 seconds. No blood points change. No sound plays.
Participants begin with 100 blood points. Points change by ±1 per outcome trial (no change on omission trials). The blood counter is displayed continuously as:
A numerical value at center screen
A colored arc on the inner circle that shrinks as blood points decrease
A heart symbol (♥) whose color shifts from green (>60 points) to yellow-orange (30–60) to red (<30 points)

The blood counter has no mechanical consequence in the current implementation — it does not end the experiment and does not trigger any qualitative state change. It functions purely as a continuous feedback display of cumulative performance, providing motivational salience to individual trial outcomes.

Scheduled breaks occur approximately every 40–60 trials (randomized to avoid episode boundary overlap). The break screen is displayed until the participant presses Space, after which an experimenter confirmation screen waits for the experimenter to press ‘P’ before the experiment resumes. Maximum break duration is 3 minutes. After each break, a drift correction is performed and a 3-2-1 countdown is shown before the next trial begins.

Within-task emotional state ratings are collected 1–2 times per episode. The rating is triggered at either trial 7 or trial 14 of the episode (randomly chosen per episode), but not both. If not triggered at trial 7 (50% chance), it is triggered at trial 14 by default.

The rating screen presents six continuous sliders (valence, arousal, anxiety, stress, frustration, fatigue) in randomized order. Participants drag the sliders with the mouse. The rating is confirmed by pressing Space.
